courses.title: IELTS Reading: Skimming & Scanning Techniques

courses.title: IELTS Reading Foundation 3.0 - 4.5

Nội dung tập trung vào cách đọc hiểu bài IELTS, nhận diện từ khóa, skimming, scanning, xử lý câu hỏi True/False/Not Given, Matching, Multiple Choice và Gap Fill. Sau khóa học, học viên có thể nắm vững phương pháp làm bài cơ bản và cải thiện tốc độ đọc hiểu.

courses.title: IELTS Speaking: Band 7.5+ Masterclass
courses.description: Succeed in your IELTS Speaking test with our advanced masterclass. Focuses on fluency, pronunciation, range of vocabulary, and coherent structuring of Parts 1, 2, and 3.

courses.title: IELTS Writing Task 1: Academic Report Guide

courses.title: IELTS Listening: High-Score Strategies
courses.description: Develop your active listening skills and build strategies for all 4 sections. Learn how to handle distractors, identify key synonyms, and complete sentences accurately.

courses.title: IELTS Reading: Speed & Accuracy Booster

tests.title: Tutor IELTS Reading Full Mock - Urban Futures
tests.description: A full 40-question Reading test created by tutor and approved by admin.

passages.title: Urban Trees and Cooler Cities
passages.content:
Cities are often warmer than the countryside around them because roads and buildings absorb heat during the day and release it slowly at night. This effect is known as the urban heat island. In recent years, city planners have turned to trees as a practical way to reduce heat and improve public health.

Trees cool streets in two main ways. Their leaves provide shade, which lowers the surface temperature of pavements and walls. They also release water vapour through a process called transpiration. When this moisture evaporates, it removes heat from the air nearby.

The benefits are not distributed equally. Wealthier districts usually have more mature trees and larger parks, while poorer districts often have fewer green spaces. Some cities now use satellite mapping to identify hot neighbourhoods and decide where new planting will have the greatest impact.

However, planting trees is not a simple cure. Young trees need watering, protection and long-term maintenance. Species must also be chosen carefully so that roots do not damage pipes and branches do not interfere with power lines. A successful urban forest is therefore a long-term infrastructure project, not a decoration.

blocks.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the information?

questions.text: Urban areas can stay warmer than nearby rural areas.
questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.text: The urban heat island effect only happens during the afternoon.
questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.text: Trees cool streets by providing shade.
questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.text: Transpiration releases water vapour from leaves.
questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.text: Satellite mapping can help planners choose planting locations.
questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.text: Young trees may need regular watering.
questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.text: Tree roots can create problems for underground pipes.
questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.text: Power lines are never affected by tree branches.
questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

questions.text: The writer describes trees as more than simple decoration.
questions.explanation: The answer follows directly from the passage wording.

passages.title: The Return of the Public Library
passages.content:
For much of the twentieth century, public libraries were mainly associated with shelves of books and silent reading rooms. Today, many libraries have become busy civic centres. They offer language classes, digital training, children s programmes and spaces where residents can work or meet.

This change has been driven partly by technology. As more public services move online, people who lack reliable internet access need a place where they can complete forms, search for jobs and learn basic computer skills. Librarians increasingly act as guides through digital systems rather than simply guardians of printed collections.

Libraries also play an important social role. In many towns, they are among the few indoor public spaces where people can spend time without buying anything. This makes them valuable for teenagers, older residents and newcomers who may not yet have strong local networks.

Funding remains a challenge. Some councils have reduced library hours or asked volunteers to run branches. Supporters argue that this approach underestimates the professional skill required to manage information, teach users and design inclusive services. The modern library may look different from its predecessor, but its core mission of public access remains unchanged.

passages.title: Microplastics in the Ocean
passages.content:
Microplastics are tiny fragments of plastic, usually smaller than five millimetres, that enter rivers and oceans from many sources. Some come from larger plastic objects that break down over time, while others are manufactured at a small size for industrial or cosmetic use.

Once in the sea, microplastics are difficult to remove. They can float near the surface, sink into sediment or be eaten by marine organisms. Researchers have found particles in fish, shellfish and even deep-sea environments once thought to be untouched by human waste.

The scientific picture is still developing. Laboratory studies suggest that some microplastics can carry chemical pollutants or cause physical stress to small organisms. Yet it is harder to measure the long-term effect in open oceans, where many variables interact.

Prevention is therefore considered more realistic than cleanup. Governments have introduced bans on certain single-use plastics, while companies are experimenting with alternative materials. Consumers can also reduce unnecessary packaging, but experts warn that individual behaviour must be supported by better product design and waste systems.

blocks.instruction: Complete the summary. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS from the passage for each answer.

questions.text: Microplastics are usually smaller than five ____.
questions.explanation: The word or phrase is taken from the passage or audio notes.

questions.text: Some fragments are produced when larger plastic objects ____ over time.
questions.explanation: The word or phrase is taken from the passage or audio notes.

questions.text: Other microplastics are made small for industrial or ____ use.
questions.explanation: The word or phrase is taken from the passage or audio notes.

questions.text: In the ocean, particles may sink into ____.
questions.explanation: The word or phrase is taken from the passage or audio notes.

questions.text: Microplastics have been discovered in fish and ____.
questions.explanation: The word or phrase is taken from the passage or audio notes.

blocks.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the information in Reading Passage 3?

Cleanup is considered more realistic than prevention.

blocks.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.

questions.text: What have governments done regarding plastic?
What are companies doing?
What can consumers do?
What do experts warn?
options: Individual behaviour must be supported by better product design and waste systems
questions.correctAnswer: Individual behaviour must be supported by better product design and waste systems

tests.title: IELTS Reading test
tests.description: bài test cải thiện kĩ năng reading cho student

passages.title: The Rise of Urban Green Spaces
passages.content:
Cities around the world have experienced rapid growth over the last century. As buildings, roads, and shopping centres replaced natural environments, many communities began to recognise the importance of preserving green spaces. Parks, gardens, and protected forests not only improve the appearance of a city but also provide important environmental and social benefits.

One of the greatest advantages of urban parks is their ability to improve air quality. Trees absorb carbon dioxide while releasing oxygen, helping reduce pollution produced by vehicles and factories. They also provide shade during hot weather, lowering temperatures and reducing the urban heat island effect.

Green spaces also support biodiversity. Birds, insects, and small mammals often find shelter in city parks where they might otherwise struggle to survive. Researchers have found that cities with larger green areas usually support a greater variety of wildlife than those with limited vegetation.

In addition to environmental benefits, parks improve public health. People who regularly visit parks are more likely to exercise through walking, cycling, or jogging. Studies suggest that spending time in nature can reduce stress, improve concentration, and support mental wellbeing.

Governments have increasingly invested in creating new public parks and restoring neglected areas. Although these projects require significant funding, many experts believe that the long-term environmental and health benefits outweigh the initial costs.

passages.title: Remote Working in Modern Businesses
passages.content:
Over the past decade, remote working has become increasingly common in many industries. Improvements in internet technology, cloud computing, and communication software have enabled employees to complete tasks from almost anywhere in the world.

Many companies adopted remote working during periods of global disruption, discovering that productivity often remained stable or even improved. Employees reported spending less time commuting, allowing them to achieve a better balance between work and personal life.

Despite these advantages, remote work also presents challenges. Some employees experience feelings of isolation because they have fewer opportunities for face-to-face interaction with colleagues. Managers have also needed to develop new methods of supervising teams and maintaining effective communication.

Businesses have invested heavily in digital collaboration tools such as video conferencing, project management software, and online document sharing. These technologies help teams coordinate work across different locations and time zones.

Experts believe that hybrid working, combining office attendance with remote work, will become the preferred model for many organisations in the future. This approach allows businesses to benefit from flexibility while maintaining opportunities for direct collaboration.

passages.title: Artificial Intelligence and Scientific Research
passages.content:
Artificial intelligence has transformed scientific research by enabling computers to analyse enormous amounts of data in a fraction of the time required by humans. Machine learning algorithms can identify patterns, predict outcomes, and assist researchers in making complex decisions.

In medicine, AI systems help doctors detect diseases earlier by analysing medical images with remarkable accuracy. Researchers also use AI to discover potential drug candidates, reducing the time needed for pharmaceutical development.

Environmental scientists rely on artificial intelligence to study climate change, monitor forests, and predict natural disasters. Satellites continuously collect large quantities of information, and AI allows scientists to process this data efficiently.

However, many experts warn that artificial intelligence should not replace human judgement entirely. Algorithms may produce inaccurate results if they are trained using incomplete or biased data. Therefore, careful human supervision remains essential throughout the research process.

As technology continues to develop, artificial intelligence is expected to become an increasingly valuable tool across nearly every scientific discipline. Rather than replacing researchers, AI is more likely to support them by performing repetitive tasks and providing deeper insights from complex datasets.

Our final destination.
Italian cuisine.
Thank you for your warm hospitality.

answers.task2: In recent years, the debate over whether automation will replace human jobs has intensified. While there are legitimate concerns about unemployment, I firmly believe that automation will ultimately create more opportunities than it destroys. Firstly, history shows that technological revolutions shift employment rather than eliminate it. During the Industrial Revolution, many feared that machines would leave the working class destitute. Instead, it gave rise to entire new industries and improved living standards. Similarly, while AI and robotics may take over repetitive tasks, they will spawn demand for skilled workers who can build, maintain, and oversee these systems. Secondly, automation can significantly enhance human productivity and well-being. By delegating mundane tasks to machines, humans can focus on creative, strategic, and interpersonal endeavors. For instance, in healthcare, automated diagnostics allow doctors to spend more time on patient care and complex treatments. The overall efficiency leads to better outcomes and a higher quality of life. In conclusion, although the transition period may be disruptive and require societal adaptation, automation is a positive force. Governments and educational institutions must focus on retraining programs to ensure that the workforce is prepared for the jobs of the future, thus reaping the full benefits of this technological advancement.

answers.task1:
The diagram illustrates the process of recycling plastic bottles, from their disposal to the production of new plastic products.

Overall, the recycling process consists of several stages, beginning with the collection of used plastic bottles and ending with the manufacture of new items. Throughout the process, waste materials are sorted, cleaned, processed into raw materials, and finally reused.

Initially, consumers place used plastic bottles into designated recycling bins. These bottles are then collected by trucks and transported to a recycling centre. At the facility, recyclable bottles are separated from non-recyclable waste before being thoroughly washed to remove any dirt or impurities.

After cleaning, the bottles are crushed into small pieces and then processed into plastic pellets. These pellets are subsequently heated and melted to form raw plastic material, which is prepared for manufacturing. In the final stage, the recycled plastic is used to produce a wide range of new products, including plastic bottles, clothing, shopping bags, stationery, and other plastic goods.

Overall, the process transforms discarded plastic bottles into valuable reusable materials, helping to reduce waste and conserve natural resources.
answers.task2:
There is an ongoing debate about whether governments should prioritise investment in public transportation or focus on improving roads and supporting private vehicles. While both approaches have their advantages, I believe that greater investment in public transport would bring more long-term benefits to society.

On the one hand, supporters of road development argue that better roads improve economic growth and make daily travel more convenient. Many people rely on private cars for commuting, particularly those living in suburban or rural areas where public transport is limited. Expanding highways and maintaining road infrastructure can reduce traffic congestion, shorten travel times, and facilitate the transportation of goods, all of which contribute to economic development.

On the other hand, investing in public transportation offers significant environmental and social benefits. Efficient systems such as buses, metro lines, and railways can carry large numbers of passengers while producing less pollution than private cars. As more people choose public transport, traffic congestion decreases, fuel consumption is reduced, and greenhouse gas emissions decline. Furthermore, affordable public transport improves accessibility for students, elderly people, and low-income citizens who may not own a vehicle.

In my opinion, governments should give priority to developing public transport while continuing to maintain existing road infrastructure. Although roads remain essential, continuously encouraging private vehicle ownership may worsen congestion and environmental problems. A modern, reliable public transport network provides a more sustainable solution for growing urban populations and contributes to a cleaner environment.

In conclusion, both public transport and road infrastructure are important. However, allocating more funding to public transportation is a wiser long-term strategy because it benefits both society and the environment while supporting sustainable economic growth.
